text: Artificial intelligence research has long been influenced by findings from the neuroscience community, with neural network and reinforcement learning research being among the many examples where knowledge derived from the brain is used to develop successful approaches to AI. However, the modern approaches to developing complex AI systems seem to be moving away from neuroscience principles and, as a result, these systems still have not yet acquired some of the basic sensorimotor skills and the more complex behavioral adaptations that even relatively simple animals develop rather effortlessly. To progress and eventually develop more sophisticated cognitive abilities in machines, we firmly believe that modern approaches to AI should move toward a new framework that is grounded in NeuroAI — a field that endeavors to extract the computational ideas encoded within the brain. Embodied Turing Test We believe it is high time for a new benchmark, namely an embodied Turing test where the ability of an embodied agent to succeed at its tasks is assessed, independent of whether a human communicator or problem solver is used as a reference, or whether the tasks involve complex thinking or communication. Our goal is to evolve the focus of AI and neuroscience research toward the evolutionally conserved core of cognition, which involves sensorimotor skills, quick responses to changing situations, the evolution of behavioral patterns through learning, efficient control of motor actions, cognitive sensing, and sensorimotion. We also believe that the current diversity of biological samples and associated associated results and data should be taken into account in developing any new computational paradigm. The potential of the approach requires the training of a new set of highly interdisciplinary experts, access to large computing facilities, as well as basic neuroscience research to enable understanding of how the entire brain functions when it is integrated into the controlling agent of a body. Passing the embodied Turing test and developing the full set of animal sensorimotor skills will provide a useful intermediary level of cognition for a machine to build upon in the development of an eventually more advanced AGI system.
